title: The Unity Editor, GameObjects, Components & Your First Scene
html:
What Unity actually is

Unity is a cross-platform engine that ships full 2D and 3D in a single package. Over this track you will use it to build everything from Pong, Breakout and Snake to a real first-person shooter, a third-person shooter with AI, and a physics-driven racing game. The first thing to install is Unity Hub from unity.com/download. The Hub manages engine versions, project lists, and licenses. Inside the Hub, open the Installs tab, click Install Editor, and choose the latest LTS (Long Term Support) release — LTS versions are stable and patched for years, which is exactly what you want while learning. During install, add the WebGL Build Support and one desktop module (Windows or Mac) so you can ship later.

Tip.
Always start projects from an LTS editor. Mixing tutorials written for different versions is the #1 source of beginner confusion. Pin one LTS version for this whole track.

Creating a project

In the Hub's Projects tab, click New project. You will see templates. The two that matter now are 2D (Built-in Render Pipeline) and 3D. The template only sets defaults (the default camera, the lighting, whether the Scene view starts in 2D mode) — it does not lock you out of the other dimension. A 3D project can still render sprites; a 2D project can still place meshes. For this first lesson choose the 3D template, name it UnityMasterclass, and pick a folder you can find again. The first launch compiles packages and may take a couple of minutes.

The Editor layout

The default window is divided into panels you will live inside for the rest of this track:

PanelWhat it does
HierarchyA list of every GameObject in the open scene, in parent/child order.
Scene viewThe 3D/2D viewport you edit in. Not the game — the workshop.
Game viewWhat the player sees through the active Camera when you press Play.
InspectorShows the components of whatever is selected, with editable fields.
ProjectEvery asset file on disk — scripts, models, textures, scenes.
ConsoleLogs, warnings and errors from your code and the engine.

Navigation in the Scene view: hold right mouse and use WASD to fly (like an FPS). Press F to frame the selected object. The colored gizmo at top-right snaps to front/top/side views, and clicking its center toggles perspective vs orthographic. The toolbar's Q W E R T Y tools are Hand, Move, Rotate, Scale, Rect, and Transform.

GameObjects and the component model

This is the single most important idea in Unity. A GameObject is an almost-empty container — it has a name, a layer, a tag, and exactly one mandatory component: a Transform (position, rotation, scale). On its own a GameObject does nothing. All behaviour comes from Components you attach: a MeshRenderer makes it visible, a Rigidbody makes it fall, a BoxCollider makes it solid, and your own MonoBehaviour scripts make it do whatever you invent. Unity favors composition over inheritance: instead of a deep class tree, you bolt small components together.

Create some objects now. Right-click in the Hierarchy, choose 3D Object > Cube, then 3D Object > Plane for a floor. Select the Cube and look at the Inspector — you will see Transform, Mesh Filter, Mesh Renderer, and Box Collider. Set its position cleanly by typing values:

Inspector — Cube
Transform Position X 0 Y 0.5 Z 0 Rotation X 0 Y 0 Z 0 Scale X 1 Y 1 Z 1

Y of 0.5 sits the 1-unit cube exactly on top of the plane (whose surface is at Y=0). One Unity unit conventionally equals one meter — keep that habit and your physics, lighting and audio falloff all behave sensibly.

Parenting and the Transform hierarchy

Drag one GameObject onto another in the Hierarchy to make it a child. Children inherit their parent's transform: move or rotate the parent and the children follow, because child coordinates are expressed relative to the parent (local space). This is how you build a car body with wheels, or a turret that rotates while its barrel stays attached. Create an empty parent with GameObject > Create Empty, name it Player, and drag the Cube under it. The Cube's Inspector now shows its local position relative to Player.

Scenes and saving

A Scene is a saved arrangement of GameObjects — a level, a menu, a loading screen. Save with Ctrl/Cmd+S; Unity asks for a name the first time. Store scenes in a Scenes folder under Assets. Everything inside Assets is your project's content on disk; the Library folder next to it is a regenerable cache you never commit to source control.

Law.
A GameObject is a bag of Components. It has no behaviour of its own. When something does not work, the question is almost always: which component is missing, disabled, or misconfigured?
Lab.
Build a tiny diorama: a plane floor, three cubes at different heights, a sphere, and a directional light (Unity adds one by default). Parent all three cubes under one empty GameObject and rotate that parent — watch them orbit together. Press Play; nothing moves yet, but confirm the Game view shows your scene through the Main Camera.
🎮 Build This: Core / Setup — Scene Sandbox

This is a concept lesson, so the Build-This is a setup exercise, not a finished genre game. It seeds the project every later game will grow from.

  1. Install Unity Hub and the latest LTS editor with WebGL + desktop modules.
  2. Create a 3D project named UnityMasterclass.
  3. Add a Plane floor at the origin and a Cube at Y=0.5 sitting on it.
  4. Create an empty GameObject named Player and parent the Cube under it.
  5. Fly around with right-mouse + WASD, press F to frame the Cube, and toggle perspective/orthographic with the gizmo.
  6. Save the scene as Sandbox in Assets/Scenes.

title: C# for Unity: MonoBehaviour, the Lifecycle, Input & Movement
html:
Scripts are components too

In the last lesson you learned a GameObject is a bag of components. A C# script that derives from MonoBehaviour is a component — once attached, Unity calls special methods on it at the right moments. Create one with Assets > Create > C# Script (or right-click in the Project window). Name it PaddleController before pressing Enter, because the filename must match the class name exactly. Unity opens it in your code editor (Visual Studio, Rider, or VS Code with the C# extension).

Danger.
If the filename and the class name disagree, Unity silently refuses to attach the script and prints Can't add script because the class can't be found. Rename one to match the other.

The MonoBehaviour lifecycle

Unity drives your code through event functions it calls automatically. You never call them yourself. The ones you will use constantly:

MethodWhen Unity calls it
Awake()Once, when the object loads — before any Start. Cache references here.
Start()Once, on the first frame the object is active — after all Awakes.
Update()Every rendered frame. Frame-rate dependent. Read input here.
FixedUpdate()Every physics step, at a fixed rate. Do physics/Rigidbody work here.
OnEnable()/OnDisable()When the component is switched on/off. Subscribe/unsubscribe events.

The key distinction: Update runs as fast as the machine renders, so anything time-based inside it must be multiplied by Time.deltaTime (the seconds since the last frame). That keeps motion consistent whether the player runs at 30 or 240 FPS.

Serialized fields: tuning from the Inspector

A public field — or a private field marked [SerializeField] — shows up in the Inspector as an editable slot. This is how designers tune values without touching code. Prefer [SerializeField] private over public: you still get the Inspector field, but other scripts cannot reach in and mutate it. Here is a complete, runnable paddle script that reads keyboard input and moves the object:

PaddleController.cs
using UnityEngine; public class PaddleController : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private float speed = 8f; [SerializeField] private float yLimit = 4f; private void Update() { // -1 .. 0 .. +1 from W/S or Up/Down float v = Input.GetAxisRaw("Vertical"); // frame-rate independent movement Vector3 delta = Vector3.up * v * speed * Time.deltaTime; Vector3 pos = transform.position + delta; // clamp inside the play field pos.y = Mathf.Clamp(pos.y, -yLimit, yLimit); transform.position = pos; } }

Several Unity idioms appear here. transform is a free reference to this object's Transform component — no lookup needed. Vector3.up is shorthand for (0,1,0). Mathf.Clamp keeps a value inside a range. Input.GetAxisRaw returns -1, 0 or +1 instantly (no smoothing) from the legacy Input Manager axes; we will graduate to the modern Input System in lesson 2.6, but the old API is perfect for a first script.

Attaching and testing

Drag the script from the Project window onto your Player GameObject in the Hierarchy (or use Add Component in the Inspector and type the name). The speed and yLimit fields now appear in the Inspector. Press Play and tap W/S or the arrow keys — the object slides and stops at the limits. Change speed while playing to feel the difference instantly. Remember: values changed during Play reset when you stop. Tune in Play, then re-enter the value while stopped to keep it.

Caching references in Awake

Calling GetComponent every frame is wasteful. Look components up once and store them:

caching pattern
private Rigidbody _rb; private void Awake() { _rb = GetComponent<Rigidbody>(); // cache once }
Tip.
Print to the Console with Debug.Log("value: " + v); to see what your code is doing. Click a log entry to jump to the exact line. This is your first and most-used debugging tool.
Law.
Read input and time-based motion in Update; always multiply by Time.deltaTime. Do Rigidbody physics in FixedUpdate. Cache component lookups in Awake.
🎮 Build This: Classic Arcade — Pong

This starts a new genre: classic arcade, beginning with Pong. It is the perfect first game because it is pure input and movement — exactly what this lesson covers.

  1. In a fresh 2D-friendly scene, set the Main Camera to Orthographic.
  2. Create two paddle quads (left and right) and a small square ball quad.
  3. Add PaddleController to the left paddle for player one.
  4. Duplicate the script as PaddleControllerP2 reading a second axis (e.g. Input.GetKey(KeyCode.O/L)) for the right paddle.
  5. Give the ball a simple Update that moves it at a constant velocity along X for now (real bounce physics arrives next lesson).
  6. Press Play and verify both paddles move and clamp inside the field.

title: 2D Physics: Rigidbody2D, Colliders, Triggers & Bounce
html:
Two physics worlds

Unity ships two independent physics engines: 3D (Box, NVIDIA PhysX) and 2D (Box2D). They do not talk to each other — a Rigidbody ignores a BoxCollider2D entirely. For our arcade games we use the 2D set, whose components all carry the 2D suffix. Switch the Scene view to 2D mode with the toolbar toggle, and make sure the Main Camera is Orthographic so there is no perspective distortion.

Rigidbody2D: who owns the motion

Add a Rigidbody2D to a GameObject and you hand control of its position to the physics engine. The most important field is Body Type:

Body TypeBehaviour
DynamicFull simulation — gravity, forces, collisions push it around. The ball.
KinematicYou move it via script; it is not pushed, but it pushes/triggers others. Paddles.
StaticNever moves. Walls and the floor. Cheapest for the engine.
Danger.
Never move a Dynamic Rigidbody by setting transform.position — you teleport it past the solver and collisions are missed (tunneling). Move dynamic bodies with rb.linearVelocity or rb.AddForce; move kinematic bodies with rb.MovePosition inside FixedUpdate.

Colliders: the shape physics actually sees

A Rigidbody handles motion; a Collider2D defines the shape that bumps into things. Common ones are BoxCollider2D, CircleCollider2D, CapsuleCollider2D, and PolygonCollider2D. Use the simplest shape that fits — a circle for a ball is both accurate and cheap. An object can have a Rigidbody without a collider (it falls through everything) or a collider without a Rigidbody (a static wall).

Physics Materials 2D: making it bounce

A pure-physics Pong ball should keep its speed and bounce perfectly. Create a Physics Material 2D (Assets > Create > 2D > Physics Material 2D), set Friction to 0 and Bounciness to 1, and drag it onto the ball collider's Material slot. Set the Rigidbody2D's Gravity Scale to 0 so the ball does not fall, and give it an initial velocity from a script:

Ball.cs
using UnityEngine; [RequireComponent(typeof(Rigidbody2D))] public class Ball : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private float speed = 7f; private Rigidbody2D _rb; private void Awake() => _rb = GetComponent<Rigidbody2D>(); private void Start() => Launch(); private void Launch() { float x = Random.value < 0.5f ? -1f : 1f; float y = Random.Range(-0.5f, 0.5f); _rb.linearVelocity = new Vector2(x, y).normalized * speed; } }

The [RequireComponent] attribute guarantees a Rigidbody2D exists, auto-adding it when you attach the script. .normalized * speed gives a consistent launch speed in a random direction. (Note: older Unity used velocity instead of linearVelocity; recent versions renamed it.)

Collision callbacks vs trigger callbacks

Unity calls methods on your script when shapes meet. There are two families, and the difference is the collider's Is Trigger checkbox:

  • Solid collisions (Is Trigger off): the engine physically stops the objects and calls OnCollisionEnter2D, OnCollisionStay2D, OnCollisionExit2D. Use for walls and paddles bouncing the ball.
  • Triggers (Is Trigger on): objects pass through each other and the engine only notifies you via OnTriggerEnter2D, OnTriggerStay2D, OnTriggerExit2D. Use for goals, pickups, kill zones.

At least one of the two objects in a pair must have a Rigidbody2D for any callback to fire. Here is a goal zone and a paddle bounce flavor:

GoalZone.cs / Ball collision
// On a trigger collider behind each paddle public class GoalZone : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private bool isLeftGoal; private void OnTriggerEnter2D(Collider2D other) { if (other.CompareTag("Ball")) ScoreManager.Instance.Score(isLeftGoal); } } // On the ball, add english based on where it hit the paddle private void OnCollisionEnter2D(Collision2D col) { if (col.collider.CompareTag("Paddle")) { float offset = transform.position.y - col.transform.position.y; Vector2 v = _rb.linearVelocity; v.y = offset * 3f; _rb.linearVelocity = v.normalized * speed; } }

Use CompareTag rather than == on .tag — it avoids a string allocation and is the idiomatic check. Assign the Ball and Paddle tags in the Inspector's Tag dropdown (add new tags via Add Tag).

Law.
Collision = engine stops them, you get OnCollision*. Trigger = they pass through, you get OnTrigger*. One object in the pair must carry a Rigidbody2D, and do physics writes in FixedUpdate.
🎮 Build This: Classic Arcade — Breakout

This starts a new genre game within classic arcade: Breakout. It reuses the bouncing-ball physics you just built for Pong and adds destructible bricks.

  1. Make the ball a Dynamic Rigidbody2D (gravity scale 0) with a CircleCollider2D and a bouncy Physics Material 2D.
  2. Add four static wall colliders around the screen edges and a kinematic paddle at the bottom using PaddleController (now reading the Horizontal axis).
  3. Lay out a grid of Brick GameObjects, each with a BoxCollider2D and a tag Brick.
  4. On the ball, in OnCollisionEnter2D, if the hit object is a Brick call Destroy(col.gameObject).
  5. Add a trigger kill-zone below the paddle that resets the ball when it falls through.
  6. Press Play and clear the wall.

title: Prefabs, Prefab Variants & Spawning at Runtime
html:
The problem prefabs solve

In Breakout you placed dozens of bricks by hand. Imagine changing their color or collider after the fact — you would edit every single one. A Prefab is a saved GameObject template stored as an asset. Every instance you drop in a scene stays linked to it, so editing the prefab updates all instances at once. Prefabs are also how you create objects at runtime: enemy waves, bullets, particles, loot. They are the backbone of almost every Unity game.

Creating a prefab

Build a GameObject the way you want it — say an Invader with a SpriteRenderer, a BoxCollider2D and an Invader.cs script — then drag it from the Hierarchy into the Project window. Unity writes a .prefab asset and the Hierarchy entry turns blue, meaning it is now an instance of that prefab. Double-click the asset (or click Open Prefab) to enter Prefab Mode, an isolated editing stage. Changes here propagate to every instance.

Overrides: instances can differ

Any instance can override properties of its prefab — drag one invader to a new position and that position is an override (shown in bold in the Inspector, with a left-margin bar). The Overrides dropdown on the instance lets you Apply a change back to the prefab (push it to all) or Revert it (snap back to the template). This apply/revert flow is what makes prefab editing safe and fast.

Prefab Variants: a child template

A Prefab Variant inherits from a base prefab but layers its own changes — like subclassing for assets. Right-click a prefab and choose Create > Prefab Variant. If your base is Invader, you can make InvaderFast (variant: higher speed) and InvaderTank (variant: more HP, different sprite). Fix a bug in the base prefab and every variant inherits the fix, while keeping their own tweaks. This is the clean way to build families of enemies, weapons or pickups.

Tip.
Prefer Variants over copy-pasting prefabs. Copies drift apart over time; variants stay synchronized with their base wherever it makes sense.

Instantiate and Destroy

To create a prefab instance from code you need a reference to the prefab asset (a serialized field) and the Instantiate method. To remove an object, call Destroy. Here is a spawner that emits a grid of invaders at start, and a projectile that destroys itself:

InvaderSpawner.cs
using UnityEngine; public class InvaderSpawner : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private GameObject invaderPrefab; [SerializeField] private int columns = 8, rows = 4; [SerializeField] private float spacing = 1.2f; private void Start() { for (int r = 0; r < rows; r++) for (int c = 0; c < columns; c++) { Vector3 pos = transform.position + new Vector3(c * spacing, -r * spacing, 0); // parent under spawner so the whole fleet can move together Instantiate(invaderPrefab, pos, Quaternion.identity, transform); } } }
Projectile.cs
public class Projectile : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private float speed = 12f; [SerializeField] private float lifeTime = 3f; private void Start() => Destroy(gameObject, lifeTime); // cleanup private void Update() => transform.Translate(Vector3.up * speed * Time.deltaTime); private void OnTriggerEnter2D(Collider2D other) { if (other.CompareTag("Invader")) { Destroy(other.gameObject); Destroy(gameObject); } } }

Instantiate takes the prefab, a position, a rotation (Quaternion.identity means "no rotation"), and optionally a parent Transform. The overload of Destroy with a delay schedules removal — handy so stray bullets do not pile up forever.

Danger.
Spawning prefabs without ever destroying them leaks objects and tanks performance. Every Instantiate needs a matching exit path: Destroy, a lifetime, or — for high-frequency objects like bullets — an object pool (covered in the twin-stick lesson). Calling Destroy in a tight loop also costs garbage collection.

The firing connection

The player ship spawns projectiles by referencing the bullet prefab and instantiating one at a muzzle point on input:

PlayerShip.cs (excerpt)
[SerializeField] private GameObject bulletPrefab; [SerializeField] private Transform muzzle; private void Update() { if (Input.GetKeyDown(KeyCode.Space)) Instantiate(bulletPrefab, muzzle.position, Quaternion.identity); }
Law.
Build it once, save it as a prefab, edit the asset to change all instances. Variants for families. Instantiate to spawn, Destroy (with a lifetime or pool) to clean up — never leak.
🎮 Build This: Classic Arcade — Space Invaders

This starts a new genre game in classic arcade: Space Invaders. It is the canonical prefab-and-spawning game — a fleet of instantiated enemies and a stream of destroyed bullets.

  1. Build one Invader GameObject (sprite + BoxCollider2D set to trigger + Invader tag) and drag it to the Project window to make a prefab.
  2. Create two variants: InvaderFast and InvaderTank, tweaking only the differing fields.
  3. Add InvaderSpawner to an empty GameObject to build the grid at Start.
  4. Make a Bullet prefab with the Projectile script and a trigger collider.
  5. Give the player ship the firing code; instantiate a bullet on Space.
  6. Press Play — shoot the fleet apart and confirm bullets and invaders are destroyed cleanly (watch the Hierarchy shrink).

title: Grids, Data Structures & Managing Game State in C#
html:
When the grid is the game

Some games are not really about physics — they are about data on a grid. Snake is the perfect example: a head that moves cell-by-cell, a body that follows, food that appears at random empty cells, and a game-over when the head hits a wall or itself. The visuals are almost incidental; the truth of the game lives in C# collections. This lesson is about modeling state cleanly in code, which pays off in every later game.

C# collections you will use constantly

TypeUse it for
T[] (array)Fixed-size data — a static grid of tiles.
List<T>Ordered, resizable — the snake's body segments.
Queue<T>First-in-first-out — input buffering, wave order.
Dictionary<K,V>Fast lookup by key — "what is at this cell?".
HashSet<T>Membership test — "is this cell occupied?" in O(1).

For Snake, a List<Vector2Int> holds the body (index 0 = head), and a HashSet<Vector2Int> mirrors it for instant self-collision tests. Vector2Int is a struct of two integers — ideal for grid coordinates because it has no floating-point error.

Ticking instead of every-frame motion

Grid games move on a tick, not every frame. We accumulate Time.deltaTime and step the simulation when enough time has passed. Input is read every frame (so it feels responsive) but only applied on the next tick. Here is the core, runnable:

SnakeGame.cs
using System.Collections.Generic; using UnityEngine; public class SnakeGame : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private int width = 20, height = 20; [SerializeField] private float tickRate = 0.15f; private readonly List<Vector2Int> _body = new(); private readonly HashSet<Vector2Int> _occupied = new(); private Vector2Int _dir = Vector2Int.right; private Vector2Int _food; private float _timer; private bool _alive = true; private void Start() { Vector2Int start = new(width / 2, height / 2); _body.Add(start); _occupied.Add(start); SpawnFood(); } private void Update() { ReadInput(); if (!_alive) return; _timer += Time.deltaTime; if (_timer >= tickRate) { _timer = 0; Tick(); } } private void ReadInput() { // block 180-degree reversals if (Input.GetKeyDown(KeyCode.W) && _dir != Vector2Int.down) _dir = Vector2Int.up; if (Input.GetKeyDown(KeyCode.S) && _dir != Vector2Int.up) _dir = Vector2Int.down; if (Input.GetKeyDown(KeyCode.A) && _dir != Vector2Int.right) _dir = Vector2Int.left; if (Input.GetKeyDown(KeyCode.D) && _dir != Vector2Int.left) _dir = Vector2Int.right; } private void Tick() { Vector2Int next = _body[0] + _dir; // wall hit or self hit = game over bool outOfBounds = next.x < 0 || next.x >= width || next.y < 0 || next.y >= height; if (outOfBounds || _occupied.Contains(next)) { GameOver(); return; } _body.Insert(0, next); _occupied.Add(next); if (next == _food) { SpawnFood(); } // grow: keep tail else { Vector2Int tail = _body[_body.Count - 1]; _body.RemoveAt(_body.Count - 1); _occupied.Remove(tail); // move: drop tail } Render(); } private void SpawnFood() { do { _food = new(Random.Range(0, width), Random.Range(0, height)); } while (_occupied.Contains(_food)); } private void GameOver() => _alive = false; }

The grow-vs-move trick

The elegant heart of Snake: every tick you insert a new head cell. If the head landed on food you stop there (the body got one longer); otherwise you remove the tail (the body kept its length but shifted forward). Keeping the HashSet in sync with the List gives O(1) self-collision tests instead of scanning the whole body every tick — a small habit that matters when the snake is long.

Rendering from data

The grid is the source of truth; visuals are a projection of it. A simple Render instantiates or repositions cube/quad markers for each body cell and the food. Because state and presentation are separated, you could swap 2D sprites for 3D cubes without touching the game logic — a clean architecture lesson that recurs throughout this track.

A central game-state manager

Bigger games need a single object that owns high-level state: score, lives, current phase (Menu, Playing, Paused, GameOver). A common pattern is a singleton manager reachable from anywhere:

GameManager.cs
public class GameManager : MonoBehaviour { public static GameManager Instance { get; private set; } public enum State { Menu, Playing, Paused, GameOver } public State Current { get; private set; } = State.Menu; public int Score { get; private set; } private void Awake() { if (Instance != null) { Destroy(gameObject); return; } Instance = this; } public void AddScore(int amount) => Score += amount; public void SetState(State s) => Current = s; }
Tip.
Singletons are convenient but easy to overuse. Use one for truly global state (the game phase, the score). For shared data we will later switch to ScriptableObjects (lesson 2.11), which decouple systems more cleanly than static references.
Law.
Model the game as data first, render second. Step grid logic on a tick, read input every frame. Keep a parallel HashSet for O(1) occupancy tests, and route high-level phase through one manager.
🎮 Build This: Classic Arcade — Snake

This starts a new genre game in classic arcade: Snake. It is data-and-state driven, the opposite of physics games, and exercises C# collections directly.

  1. Add SnakeGame to an empty GameObject and set a 20x20 grid.
  2. Write Render() to instantiate a cube/quad per body cell and one for the food (reuse a small pool or destroy+respawn markers each tick).
  3. Verify growth: eating food keeps the tail, normal moves drop it.
  4. Confirm self-collision and wall-collision both trigger GameOver.
  5. Add a GameManager singleton, call AddScore(1) on each food eaten, and show the score in the Console for now.
  6. Tune tickRate down as the score rises for escalating difficulty.

title: The New Input System: Actions, Maps & Multi-Device Control
html:
Why move off the old Input Manager

So far we used Input.GetAxis and Input.GetKeyDown — the legacy Input Manager. It is simple but limited: bindings are buried in Project Settings, it does not cleanly support gamepads or rebinding, and it cannot easily handle local multiplayer or device hot-swapping. Unity's modern Input System package fixes all of this with a data-driven asset of Actions and Bindings. Asteroids — thrust, rotate, fire, hyperspace, on keyboard and gamepad — is the ideal showcase.

Installing and enabling it

Open Window > Package Manager, switch to the Unity Registry, find Input System, and Install. Unity prompts to set the Active Input Handling to the new (or Both) backend and restart the editor. Set it to Input System Package (New) or Both while you migrate. After restart, the old Input API may stop working depending on which mode you chose — that is expected.

The core concepts

ConceptMeaning
ActionAn intent: "Move", "Fire", "Thrust". Device-agnostic.
BindingA concrete control mapped to an action: <Keyboard>/space, <Gamepad>/buttonSouth.
Action MapA group of actions for a context: "Gameplay", "UI", "Menu".
Control SchemeA device set (Keyboard&Mouse, Gamepad) the maps can switch between.
Action TypeValue (continuous, e.g. movement), Button (pressed), or Pass-Through.

Creating the Input Actions asset

Right-click in the Project window and choose Create > Input Actions. Name it PlayerControls and double-click to open the editor. Add an action map Gameplay. Inside it add actions: Move (Value, Vector2), Fire (Button), Thrust (Button). For Move, add a 2D Vector composite binding (Up/Down/Left/Right) bound to WASD, plus a separate binding to the gamepad's <Gamepad>/leftStick. For Fire, bind <Keyboard>/space and <Gamepad>/buttonSouth. Check Generate C# Class in the asset's Inspector and Apply — Unity writes a strongly-typed wrapper you can use from code.

Tip.
One action can have many bindings across devices. The Input System merges them, so "Fire" works on space, the gamepad A button, and a mouse click simultaneously with zero extra code.

Reading actions in code

The cleanest path is the generated class plus callbacks. Subscribe in OnEnable, unsubscribe in OnDisable:

AsteroidsShip.cs
using UnityEngine; using UnityEngine.InputSystem; [RequireComponent(typeof(Rigidbody2D))] public class AsteroidsShip : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private float thrust = 6f, turn = 200f; [SerializeField] private GameObject bulletPrefab; [SerializeField] private Transform muzzle; private PlayerControls _controls; private Rigidbody2D _rb; private Vector2 _move; private void Awake() { _rb = GetComponent<Rigidbody2D>(); _controls = new PlayerControls(); } private void OnEnable() { _controls.Gameplay.Enable(); _controls.Gameplay.Fire.performed += OnFire; } private void OnDisable() { _controls.Gameplay.Fire.performed -= OnFire; _controls.Gameplay.Disable(); } private void Update() { _move = _controls.Gameplay.Move.ReadValue<Vector2>(); // rotate from horizontal input transform.Rotate(0, 0, -_move.x * turn * Time.deltaTime); } private void FixedUpdate() { if (_move.y > 0.1f) _rb.AddForce(transform.up * thrust); // classic inertia thrust } private void OnFire(InputAction.CallbackContext ctx) => Instantiate(bulletPrefab, muzzle.position, transform.rotation); }

Note the division of labor: ReadValue polls a continuous Value action in Update; the Button action fires a one-shot performed callback so you do not need to detect edges yourself. Physics (AddForce) stays in FixedUpdate.

Danger.
Forgetting to Enable() the action map means every read returns zero and nothing responds — a classic silent failure. Always enable in OnEnable and disable in OnDisable, and unsubscribe callbacks to avoid leaks when objects are destroyed.

Screen wrap-around

Asteroids' signature is toroidal space: leave one edge, appear on the opposite one. Convert position to viewport space and flip when it crosses 0 or 1:

ScreenWrap.cs
public class ScreenWrap : MonoBehaviour { private Camera _cam; private void Awake() => _cam = Camera.main; private void LateUpdate() { Vector3 vp = _cam.WorldToViewportPoint(transform.position); if (vp.x < 0) vp.x = 1; else if (vp.x > 1) vp.x = 0; if (vp.y < 0) vp.y = 1; else if (vp.y > 1) vp.y = 0; transform.position = _cam.ViewportToWorldPoint(vp); } }
Law.
Define device-agnostic Actions, bind many devices to each, generate the C# class, and enable/disable the map with the object's lifecycle. Poll Value actions in Update; handle Button actions via the performed callback.
🎮 Build This: Classic Arcade — Asteroids

This starts a new genre game in classic arcade: Asteroids. It is built around the new Input System and inertia movement, and reuses the prefab spawning from Space Invaders for bullets and asteroids.

  1. Install the Input System package and set Active Input Handling to Both.
  2. Create the PlayerControls asset with a Gameplay map: Move (Vector2, WASD + left stick), Fire and Thrust (button, keyboard + gamepad). Generate the C# class.
  3. Build a ship with a Rigidbody2D (gravity 0, low linear/angular drag) and attach AsteroidsShip and ScreenWrap.
  4. Make a Bullet prefab (also with ScreenWrap or a lifetime) and large/medium/small Asteroid prefabs as variants.
  5. On bullet-asteroid hit, destroy both and spawn two smaller asteroid variants.
  6. Play with both keyboard and a connected gamepad to confirm multi-device control and wrap-around.

title: 2D Platformer Movement: Gravity, Jump Feel & Ground Checks
html:
Game feel is engineered, not lucky

A platformer lives or dies on how its jump feels. That feel is not an accident — it is a stack of deliberate tricks: tuned gravity, a ground check, variable jump height, coyote time, and a jump buffer. We will build a responsive controller from scratch and understand every knob. This starts a new genre: the side-scrolling platformer.

Why we control gravity ourselves

The default Rigidbody2D gravity gives a floaty, symmetric arc. Great platformers fall faster than they rise and let the player cut a jump short by releasing the button. The classic technique: use a base gravity, then multiply it when falling, and multiply it more when rising with the button released. We apply this by setting gravityScale dynamically or adding velocity ourselves. Set the Rigidbody2D to Dynamic, freeze its Z rotation (so the character cannot tip over), and set Collision Detection to Continuous to avoid tunneling through thin platforms at speed.

Ground checks with a raycast

You can only jump if you are on the ground. The robust way to know is a short raycast (or BoxCast) downward against a Ground layer. Create a layer named Ground, assign it to your platforms, and expose a LayerMask so the check ignores everything else. A BoxCast matching the player's width avoids edge cases where a single ray misses a ledge corner.

Tip.
Prefer a BoxCast or two-corner rays over a single center ray. A lone ray straight down reports "airborne" the instant the player's center leaves a platform edge, causing missed jumps at ledges.

The full controller

PlatformerController.cs
using UnityEngine; [RequireComponent(typeof(Rigidbody2D))] public class PlatformerController : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private float moveSpeed = 8f; [SerializeField] private float jumpForce = 15f; [SerializeField] private float fallMultiplier = 2.5f; [SerializeField] private float lowJumpMultiplier = 2f; [SerializeField] private float coyoteTime = 0.1f; [SerializeField] private float jumpBuffer = 0.1f; [SerializeField] private LayerMask groundLayer; [SerializeField] private Vector2 boxSize = new(0.8f, 0.1f); [SerializeField] private float castDistance = 0.5f; private Rigidbody2D _rb; private float _moveInput; private float _coyoteCounter; private float _bufferCounter; private bool _jumpHeld; private void Awake() => _rb = GetComponent<Rigidbody2D>(); private bool IsGrounded() => Physics2D.BoxCast(transform.position, boxSize, 0f, Vector2.down, castDistance, groundLayer).collider != null; private void Update() { _moveInput = Input.GetAxisRaw("Horizontal"); _jumpHeld = Input.GetButton("Jump"); // coyote time: brief grace after leaving ground if (IsGrounded()) _coyoteCounter = coyoteTime; else _coyoteCounter -= Time.deltaTime; // jump buffer: remember a press made just before landing if (Input.GetButtonDown("Jump")) _bufferCounter = jumpBuffer; else _bufferCounter -= Time.deltaTime; if (_bufferCounter > 0 && _coyoteCounter > 0) { _rb.linearVelocity = new Vector2(_rb.linearVelocity.x, jumpForce); _bufferCounter = 0; _coyoteCounter = 0; } } private void FixedUpdate() { _rb.linearVelocity = new Vector2(_moveInput * moveSpeed, _rb.linearVelocity.y); ApplyBetterGravity(); } private void ApplyBetterGravity() { if (_rb.linearVelocity.y < 0) _rb.linearVelocity += Vector2.up * Physics2D.gravity.y * (fallMultiplier - 1) * Time.fixedDeltaTime; else if (_rb.linearVelocity.y > 0 && !_jumpHeld) _rb.linearVelocity += Vector2.up * Physics2D.gravity.y * (lowJumpMultiplier - 1) * Time.fixedDeltaTime; } }

Decoding the feel knobs

KnobEffect on feel
fallMultiplierHigher = snappier, weightier fall. The arc stops floating.
lowJumpMultiplierVariable jump: tap = short hop, hold = full height.
coyoteTimeForgives jumps pressed a few frames after walking off a ledge.
jumpBufferHonors jumps pressed a few frames before landing.

Coyote time and jump buffering are invisible when they work — players just feel the game is "responsive" and "fair." Without them the same player feels the controls are "sticky" or "dropped my input," even though the code without them is technically correct. This is the essence of game feel: serving the player's intent, not just their literal input timing.

Danger.
Do not set velocity in Update — physics writes belong in FixedUpdate, or motion stutters and depends on frame rate. Read input (which is edge-sensitive) in Update, store it, and apply it in FixedUpdate, exactly as above.
Lab.
With the controller attached and a couple of ground platforms in place, tune live: set fallMultiplier to 1 and feel the floatiness, then raise it to 2.5. Set coyoteTime to 0 and try to jump off a ledge edge — notice the missed jumps — then restore it to 0.1 and feel the difference.
Law.
Great jumps = tuned asymmetric gravity + variable height + coyote time + jump buffer + a forgiving (box) ground check. Read input in Update, move in FixedUpdate.
🎮 Build This: Side-Scrolling Platformer — Movement Core

This starts a new genre: the side-scrolling platformer. We build the movement core now; the next lesson paints real levels around it with tilemaps.

  1. Create a player GameObject with a SpriteRenderer, a BoxCollider2D (or CapsuleCollider2D), and a Dynamic Rigidbody2D with frozen Z rotation.
  2. Add a Ground layer and assign it to a few platform GameObjects with colliders.
  3. Attach PlatformerController and set its groundLayer mask to Ground.
  4. In the Input Manager confirm the Horizontal and Jump axes exist (they do by default).
  5. Play and tune the four feel knobs until the jump feels crisp and weighty.
  6. Test coyote time by jumping just after leaving a ledge, and the buffer by pressing jump just before landing.

title: Tilemap, Tile Palettes, Composite Colliders & Level Building
html:
From hand-placed platforms to painted levels

In the last lesson you dropped a few platforms by hand. Real platformer levels have hundreds of pieces — placing each as a GameObject would be unmanageable and slow. Unity's Tilemap system lets you paint levels from a palette of tiles, stores them efficiently, and generates collision automatically. This lesson extends the side-scrolling platformer you started, giving its movement core a proper world to run through.

The Tilemap component family

PieceRole
GridThe parent that defines cell size and layout. Tilemaps live under it.
TilemapA layer of painted tiles (e.g. Ground, Background, Hazards).
Tilemap RendererDraws the tilemap, batching many tiles into few draw calls.
Tile PaletteThe editor window holding the brushes you paint with.
Tile assetA reusable tile referencing a sprite; Rule Tiles add auto-logic.

Create the base with GameObject > 2D Object > Tilemap > Rectangular. Unity makes a Grid with a child Tilemap. Add several child tilemaps for separate layers — keep collidable terrain on its own tilemap so collision stays clean. Set the Sorting Layer / Order in Layer per tilemap so the background draws behind the player and the foreground in front.

Slicing a sprite sheet and building a palette

Import a tileset PNG. In its import settings set Sprite Mode to Multiple, set Pixels Per Unit to match your art (commonly 16 or 32), and set Filter Mode to Point (no filter) and Compression to None for crisp pixel art. Open the Sprite Editor and use Slice > Grid By Cell Size to cut it into individual tiles. Then open Window > 2D > Tile Palette, create a new palette, and drag the sliced sprites into it — Unity generates Tile assets for you. Pick the brush and paint directly into the Scene view onto the active tilemap (choose which tilemap is active in the palette's dropdown).

Tip.
Hold the right keys while painting: the palette toolbar has Select, Move, Brush, Box-fill, Picker (eyedropper), and Erase. Box-fill lays large platforms instantly; the eyedropper grabs a tile already in the scene to keep painting with it.

Rule Tiles: smart auto-tiling

Painting individual corner, edge and middle tiles by hand is tedious and error-prone. A Rule Tile (from the free 2D Tilemap Extras package, installed via Package Manager) chooses the correct sprite automatically based on its neighbors. Create one with Create > 2D > Tiles > Rule Tile, assign a default sprite, then add rules: for each neighbor pattern ("this side has a tile / must not have a tile") pick the matching edge or corner sprite. Now paint with the single Rule Tile and the grass tops, dirt edges and inner corners resolve themselves as you draw. This is how production 2D levels are built quickly and consistently.

Efficient collision with a Composite Collider

Each terrain tile could get its own collider, but that creates thousands of tiny colliders and seams the player can snag on. The right approach is three components on the collidable tilemap:

  • Tilemap Collider 2D — generates a collider following the painted tiles.
  • Composite Collider 2D — merges all those shapes into one optimized outline.
  • Rigidbody2D set to Static — required by the Composite Collider.

On the Tilemap Collider 2D, enable Used By Composite. The result is a single smooth surface with far fewer physics shapes — better performance and no snagging on internal tile edges. Make sure this tilemap's GameObject is on the Ground layer so the platformer controller's BoxCast ground check (from lesson 2.7) detects it.

Ground Tilemap — component stack
GameObject: Tilemap_Ground (Layer: Ground) Tilemap Tilemap Renderer Sorting Layer: Terrain Tilemap Collider 2D Used By Composite: true Composite Collider 2D Geometry Type: Outlines Rigidbody 2D Body Type: Static

Querying and editing tiles from code

Tilemaps are not just art — you can read and write them at runtime for destructible terrain, secret passages, or procedural levels (used heavily in the roguelike lesson). The API works in cell coordinates:

TileEditing.cs
using UnityEngine; using UnityEngine.Tilemaps; public class TileEditing : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private Tilemap tilemap; [SerializeField] private TileBase replacement; public void DestroyTileAt(Vector3 worldPos) { Vector3Int cell = tilemap.WorldToCell(worldPos); if (tilemap.HasTile(cell)) tilemap.SetTile(cell, null); // null clears the cell } public void PaintTileAt(Vector3 worldPos) { Vector3Int cell = tilemap.WorldToCell(worldPos); tilemap.SetTile(cell, replacement); } }

WorldToCell converts a world position (say, where a bullet hit) into grid coordinates; SetTile with null erases it, and the Composite Collider rebuilds its outline automatically.

Danger.
If your character clips through painted ground, the usual cause is a missing Static Rigidbody2D on the composite tilemap, Used By Composite left off the Tilemap Collider, or mismatched Pixels Per Unit between the tileset and the rest of your art, which throws off the collider scale.
Law.
Paint with a palette (and Rule Tiles for speed), keep collidable terrain on its own tilemap, and collapse its collision with Tilemap Collider 2D + Composite Collider 2D + Static Rigidbody2D on the Ground layer.
🎮 Build This: Side-Scrolling Platformer — Level Building

This extends the side-scrolling platformer from lesson 2.7, building real levels for the movement core you already have.

  1. Import a tileset, slice it in the Sprite Editor (Point filter, no compression, matching PPU).
  2. Create a Grid with separate Background, Terrain, and Hazard tilemaps.
  3. Make a Tile Palette, drag in the sprites, and build a Rule Tile for the ground so edges auto-resolve.
  4. Paint a level with platforms, gaps, and a few floating ledges.
  5. On the Terrain tilemap add Tilemap Collider 2D (Used By Composite), Composite Collider 2D, and a Static Rigidbody2D; put it on the Ground layer.
  6. Drop your PlatformerController player in and run the level — confirm the BoxCast ground check works against the composite collider, and that coyote time still feels right at ledge edges.
